---
title: Shiptons Heating, Cooling, Unnecessary Repair, Ransom, Bullying and Blackmail Company
author: Jeffrey S. Racine
date: 2024-12-21
draft: false
feed: true
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---


File this post under the "fool me three times, shame on me" category.


# Are Guerrilla Business Tactics the New Norm?

Our home has two furnaces and one A/C unit. They were updated in 2005 when we bought our home, and we purchased well built energy efficient units designed to last. We prefer to have our equipment inspected and serviced regularly before problems arise, rather than waiting for something to go wrong then address the underlying issue. As well, we would rather maintain equipment that is serviceable for as long as possible instead of dumping it in a landfill prematurely.

So, we took out a service contract that promised savings by bundling the three units, and we pay $29/month (we provide the replacement humidifier pads and charcoal filters). This contract covers annual maintenance for each unit, and the units have been maintained and inspected annually and have performed admirably over the years. 

The service contract company (Shiptons Heating and Cooling) had a good reputation we were told (a decade ago that is), and they generally show up as scheduled, test the units, replace humidifier pads and clean and service the units annually. We were told that good equipment, well maintained, can last far beyond the average life expectancy often reported by the industry.

Unfortunately, we have come to realize that this particular service contract company employs what might be described as "guerrilla business tactics", and I penned this post to link to in my online reviews of the company for those interested in the details.

## Strike One (Unnecessary Repairs)

In October 2023 during the routine maintenance of our furnaces, the tech who was dispatched (Peter Tambeau) told us the unit was showing its age, and in the written summary of his visit wrote "immediate repair, replace collector box". 

I contacted Shiptons dispatch and was told "I can confirm the price of the Collector Box is $417.60+H.S.T. (which includes the installation). [] As discussed, upon arrival of the part, I'll be directly in touch with Mrs. Racine & yourself to coordinate a date/time for the repair.  Please don't hesitate in contacting us if we can be of any further assistance in the interim."

Here is my email to Shiptons after Pete showed up at the house to do the repair:

> This morning the tech arrived to install the collector box we were advised to fix (the same tech who serviced the unit recently and wrote on the attached summary “immediate repair” Collector Box $417+HST). When he arrived he said “I don’t know why I am here, replacing the collector box won’t fix the leak in the unit”… I then said another tech had come and inspected the unit to get the part number, and he said “no one talked to me, I noted the part number.”  

> I asked him why he wrote “immediate repair collector box” and his reply was “they tell us to write something”. In other words, he is claiming that the collector box did not have a leak yet he wrote that this should be repaired immediately. 

> This is unacceptable. [] Shiptons has dispatched two techs to deal with an issue clearly indicated as “Immediate repair” wasting money on two trips not to mention our time and frustration. The purpose of this email is to request that, going forward, kindly put a note on our file to not dispatch this technician to our residence (Peter Tambeau). 

> Thanks for your understanding.

So, chalk this visit up to "unnecessary repairs recommended"...

*P.S., the unit continues to perform flawlessly.*

## Strike Two (Ransom)

In June 2024 a technician was dispatched for the annual maintenance of the A/C unit (Stephen Malgo). 

At the end of the service call, and with zero prior discussion, Steven said he had completed the maintenance, had disabled the A/C from the exterior of the house, and would arrange for Scott Shipton to come to our house that afternoon to discuss replacement options. The A/C unit had been operating perfectly (and, it still does for the record). I was stunned, and told him the unit works fine. He said it is low on refrigerant and that it could freeze up and leak. I said Shiptons installed *two* leak prevention automatic shut off devices to prevent leak damage (one redundant), which he was aware of. I instructed him to go outside and enable the A/C unit. 

We did not receive a call from Scott Shipton to schedule a visit, but the next day were told someone else (Daniel Arcari) came to the house unannounced and we were not home, so he left. Daniel's visit was  rescheduled for the following day, and on his second visit said the unit was old, was low on refrigerant and could not be recharged so our only option was to replace. I asked for a quote and weeks later got the following email:

> "Hey Jeffrey, I spoke with Scott and we figured out more or less what needs to be done. The cost to replace the 3 ton air conditioner and the air handler unit is. $15,395 plus HST". 

On July 3 2024 I replied to Daniel's quote writing 

> "Would you kindly send the details for the quote you kindly provided? We were expecting more than a simple number, in particular the make/model of the A/C and air handler to be installed, cost of all other parts needed for the installation, and a breakdown of the $15,395 total into parts and labor, etc."

Crickets... zero response to date.

So, chalk this visit up to "we hijacked your A/C unit by disabling it and are seeking ransom in the amount of the cost of replacement"...

*P.S., a competitor said they could recharge the unit, no problem, and though we did not recharge it yet it continues to work like a champ.*

## Strike Three (Bullying and Blackmail)

In December 2024 we had our annual furnace maintenance. 

At the end of his visit, on the invoice he presented, the technician (Josh Pattynama) wrote "immediate repair: 1) collector box ($470.25+HST), 2) venting repair ($1,399+HST), and 3) it may be time to have a home comfort adviser provide options on a more efficient system". He also wrote "Furnace 1 has mixed venting that is against code". 

Let me remind you that 1) is the exact same issue a different tech (Pete) reported last year (“they tell us to write something”) and that when the tech (Pete) was dispatched last year with the collector box to do the repair told us “I don’t know why I am here, replacing the collector box won’t fix the leak in the unit”. So, naturally, we have some reason to be suspicious at this stage.

Now, Shiptons has maintained these systems for the past 20 years, and we have been told in the past that some venting is not up to current code but that we are grandfathered in (many homes are obviously in a similar situation). We were also advised in the past that some of the vent pipe is not up to code and can crack and create a hazard and should be replaced with a different material, which we did, willingly and gratefully.

But then, out of the blue, the tech (Josh) produces a "Safety Violation Notice" giving us 42 days to complete the repairs, stating "If not corrected, the gas supply will be discontinued on DUE DATE and charges may apply". And have the repairs completed by whom you might ask? 

I was out of the country and the tech (Josh) was appraised of the fact that I was called away to deal with a family emergency. He obviously saw we had renovations going on and that the house was full of trades who were calling on my wife for various decisions, yet Josh insisted my wife sign the document on the spot and would not leave until she did. She said we would like time to think about this and get a second opinion as she had her hands full at the moment as he could plainly see, reminding him I had been called away on an emergency. She said that it was always our understanding that we were grandfathered in, that Shiptons has been maintaining our systems for 20 years, and that this sounds rather extreme, so can this not wait until my return? He refused her request for either and said he would not leave until she signed. This type of bullying behaviour is simply unacceptable.

In order to get Josh off of our property (recall we are in the midst of renovations), my wife had our contractor take time out of his day to go into the basement with him to review the issue, and only after that did Josh leave saying that he might "revisit this in the New Year".

Note the violation clearly states "The appliance and/or piping does not constitute an immediate hazard", so there was absolutely no reason to deny us time to discuss this issue upon my return.

A scan of the front page of notice, and the filled in document we refused to sign are below, you can scroll them to view them in their entirety:

::: {layout-row="2"}
![](images/notice.pdf){width="400"}

![](images/form.pdf){width="400"}
:::



I then sent the following to Shiptons three days ago:

> Greetings [],

> After the third service visit in a row (specifically, scheduled maintenance last Fall, this Spring, and now this Fall) that were some of the most unpleasant experiences my wife or I have had with any company, ever, we are left with no choice but to terminate our longstanding relationship with your company.
 
> Kindly cancel our service agreement, the automatic monthly withdrawals from our account, and also please send confirmation of so doing at year earliest convenience.
 
> Thanks for your understanding, I look forward to receiving confirmation and we appreciate your timely attention to this unpleasant matter.
 
So far, crickets...

*P.S., though it "does not constitute an immediate hazard", I have zero desire to live with unsafe equipment,  and am revisiting this issue in the New Year with a company that doesn't base their business model on unnecessary repairs, bullying, ransom and blackmail (today is Saturday, next week is Christmas then New Years after that).*

# Epilogue

In retrospect, warning signs about dealing with this company had surfaced multiple times, and it is on us for not acting sooner. For example, 

  - A few years ago a tech was dispatched by Shiptons to do the maintenance. I asked how Shiptons was treating them and they said they were going to work for another company. They added without any prompting that we have really good equipment, and that Shiptons advises the techs to encourage clients to upgrade their equipment prematurely which was one reason they were leaving. 
  
  - A former employee (not the one just mentioned) told us the owners and management at Shiptons use high pressure tactics and financially reward techs who successfully generate sales. 
  
  - After the most recent event, a company recommended by our contractor examined the offending equipment, and guess what they noticed upon inspecting the furnaces? That the furnaces were dirty and could use a good cleaning! When we said the furnaces had recently been cleaned they offered to take photos to send to the complaints department of the company that had done the cleaning if we wanted. And when did this new inspection occur, you might ask?  The afternoon of the same day on which Shiptons had done their annual maintenance and cleaning, i.e., just a few hours apart!
  
Enough said.
